sentences: Dr. Jekyll was very calm.
There was a murder case, a letter, and an incident with Dr. Lanyon.
There was also a story about a window and the last night.
Dr. Lanyon told his story, and Dr. Jekyll wrote a full report.
Mr. Utterson, the lawyer, had a tough face and never smiled.
He was quiet, didn't talk much, and was slow to show feelings.
He was tall, thin, and looked tired, but people liked him.
When with friends and wine, his eyes showed warmth he didn't say in words.
This warmth showed in what he did too.
He drank gin alone to not want better drinks.
He liked the theater but hadn't gone in twenty years.
But, he was kind to others, even if he was a bit jealous of their fun.
He always chose to help, not to judge.
"I think like Cain," he often said.
"I let my brother make his own mistakes." He helped men who were struggling.
As long as they came to his office, he was nice to them.
Utterson didn't show feelings much, but he was kind in his way.
He was humble and happy with the friends he had.
His friends were family or old friends; he grew to like them over time.
This is why he was close to Mr. Richard Enfield, a known social person.
Many people wondered what these two saw in each other or what they could possibly have in common.
Those who saw them on their Sunday walks reported that they didn't talk much, looked bored, and seemed relieved when they ran into a friend.
Despite this, they both valued these outings, considered them the highlight of their week, and would even put off work or other enjoyable activities to make sure they weren't interrupted.
One day, during one of these walks, they found themselves in a side street in a busy part of London.
The street was small and usually quiet, but it was bustling with business during the week.
The residents all seemed to be doing well, hoping to do even better, and spending their extra earnings on making their shops look attractive.
The shops lined the street, inviting customers in like rows of smiling saleswomen.
IMPORTANT: Keep Stevenson's dark Victorian atmosphere while making the language more modern and accessible.
Even on Sunday, when it hid its more vibrant allure and was relatively empty, the street stood out against its drab surroundings, like a fire in a forest.
Its freshly painted shutters, well-polished brass fixtures, and overall cleanliness and cheerfulness, immediately caught and delighted the eye of passersby.
Two doors from one corner, on the left side heading east, the line of buildings was interrupted by the entrance to a courtyard.
At this point, a particularly ominous block of building jutted out onto the street.
It was two stories high, with no windows, just a door on the lower floor and a blank, discolored wall above.
Every aspect of it showed signs of long-term, grimy neglect.
The door, which had neither a bell nor a knocker, was weathered and stained.
Homeless people lounged in the doorway, striking matches on the panels.
Children played shop on the steps.
Schoolboys had tested their knives on the moldings.
For nearly a generation, no one had bothered to chase away these random visitors or fix the damage they caused.
Enfield and the lawyer were on the other side of the street, but when they lined up with the entrance, Enfield pointed with his cane.
He asked if I had ever seen that door.
When I said yes, he told me it reminded him of a strange story.
Mr. Utterson's voice sounded different when he asked, 'Really?'
'What was it?' he wanted to know.
'It happened like this,' Mr. Enfield started.
I was coming back from the city's edge at 3 a.m. on a dark winter night.
I walked through a part of town where only street lamps were on.
Street after street was lit up but empty, like everyone was asleep.
I wished I would see a police officer while walking.
Suddenly, I saw two people: a small man and a young girl running.
They ran into each other at the corner.
Then, something scary happened.
The man walked over the girl and left her screaming on the ground.
It might not sound bad, but seeing it was terrible.
It didn't seem human; it was like a monster.
I yelled, chased him, caught him, and took him back to the crowd around the girl.
He was calm and looked at me in a way that scared me.
The crowd was the girl's family.
A doctor came soon and said the girl was scared but not badly hurt.
You would think that was the end, but it wasn't.
I did not like the man at all.
The child's family didn't like him either, which made sense.
Remember to keep the story dark but easy to understand.
But the doctor's reaction was very interesting to me.
He was a normal pharmacist, with no special look, and spoke with a strong accent.
Every time he saw my prisoner, he looked scared and angry.
I knew what he was thinking, and he knew my thoughts, so we did something else.
We scared the man with a big scandal that could destroy his name in London.
If he had friends or respect, we said he would lose them.
While we scared him, we tried to keep the women away because they were very angry.
I saw many angry faces, and the man was calm but looked scared, acting very cool.
He said, 'If you use this against me, I can't stop you.
He said, 'No real gentleman wants to make a scene.'
He asked, 'What do you want?' We made him pay a hundred pounds for the child's family.
Next, we had to get the money, and he took us to a surprising place.
He used a key, went inside, and came back with gold and a check from a famous bank.
The money was a lot, but the signature on the check was very important.
I told the man it was strange to get so much money at four in the morning.
He didn't worry about it at all.
He said, 'Don't worry, I'll go with you to the bank.' So we waited together and went to the bank after breakfast.
I gave the check myself, and I thought it was fake.
The check was real.
"Wow!" said Mr.
"The man was very bad, and the one who wrote the check is very good and famous for helping others."
"Maybe it's blackmail, where a good man pays for old mistakes," he said.
I call that place the 'Black Mail House'.
"But that doesn't explain everything," he said, thinking hard.
Utterson asked, "Do you know if the check writer lives there?" "It seems likely," said Mr.
"I saw his address; he lives in a square," said Mr. "And you didn't ask about the door?" asked Mr.
"No, sir; I thought it wasn't right," was the answer.
"Asking questions feels wrong, like it's too important," he said.
"When you ask, it's like starting something big without meaning to."
"You do nothing, and suddenly, someone innocent gets hurt and their family is affected," he said.
"No sir, if it's strange, I don't ask," he said. "That's smart," said the lawyer.
"But I looked at the place myself," Mr. continued.
"There's one door, and only the man from my story uses it."
"There are three windows on the first floor facing the yard, all shut but clean."
"And there's a chimney that smokes, so someone lives there."
"But it's hard to tell, the buildings are so close together," he said.
They were quiet, then Mr. Utterson said, "Enfield, that's a smart rule." "Yes, I think so," Enfield replied.
